chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: So today I want to talk with you about how it is that we can do research of a scientific

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: kind for something as complex as human behavior, as opposed to things that you measure out in

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: space or test tubes or under microscopes.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: People are complex organisms, right?

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: And so to do research on them to understand human nature, how the mind works, how the brain

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: supports it, is a challenging story.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: So, the usual thing is an experiment.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: You have a group of participants, we'll talk about that.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: But in the reading for today, and I know for the first time we've done this, so you may

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: not have read it, but if you have that be great, is it kind of another thread of the kind of

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: a way that people are studied, which is dramatic individual stories, case studies, single stories

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: that are illustrative of something that we think are broader principles.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: And a lot of our biggest discoveries, and we'll come to that later in the course, have been

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: made on very unusual, single individuals who turn out to have a lesson that's pretty

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: broad, for example, for the organization of the human brain.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: So in the Oliver Sacks book, there's a story about Donald, and what did Donald do that

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: got him in big trouble?

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: Yeah, he murdered his girlfriend while he was on PCP.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: He's in a psychiatric hospital.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: They try hypnosis to help him remember it.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: He says he doesn't remember it.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: Many of you may know that an astounding number of criminals under interrogation tell you

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: they don't remember anything about the episode involved, right?

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: So people are suspicious at first for many reasons, because these things just say, I don't

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: remember what happened, and then you can't catch yourself in a lie or any complexity,

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: right?

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: Because you don't remember what happened.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: And sometimes you don't remember, and sometimes you do.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: But in this case, they try hypnosis.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: That doesn't help.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: He's in there for four years.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: And then he knows, conceptually, that he murdered his girlfriend, and he says, I'm not fit

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: for society.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: He knows it's a terrible thing he did.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: But it's kind of like an intellectual knowledge, right?

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: It's not a feeling, it's not a memory.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: And then he goes biking and something happens, which is, he gets hit by a car.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: And so there's what appears to be a drug-induced amnesia of the original murderous episode.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: He gets hit by a car, he has a severe head injury, he's in a coma for two weeks.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: He's left-sided weakness as he comes out of it, which means the biggest injury was in

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: the right side of his brain.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: He has an numbness, and he has frontal lobe contusions.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: He has injury and bleeding around the frontal lobes that cortex that sits in the front of

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: the brain.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: But now a remarkable thing happens is that two insults, two wrongs made a right.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: The first wrong being the PCP, the second wrong being the accident, and now what happens?

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: He remembers, in vivid and florid detail, the horror that he actually committed this murder.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: It's just two insults for the brain.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: One drug-induced, one of focal injury repressed the memory and an unrepress the memory.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: And one of the first things I have to do is discover whether he really has his memory or

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: he just thinks he has his memory.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: And how do they figure out to their satisfaction whether it's a really recovered memory or a fabrication

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: of his mind?

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: Right, that's really important.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: He remembers details of the murder that are not publicly available, that were not in the

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: information that was given to him.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: That's a really huge issue in these kinds of memories.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: So they said, really recovered memory, not an inadvertently fabricated memory.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: And they discover he has deep seizure activity in his limbic structures, the parts of his brain

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: that are involved in emotion are having seizures.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: So in epilepsy, neurons are firing for no good reason.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: And that's part of his disorder.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: They give him medications to help with that.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: But here's the question for you for a moment to think about.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: And it's now such a common question because our society is moving that way that it's on

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: many TV shows.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: I see it like every couple of weeks, there's a law and order episode that has something like

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: this, which is the more you know about the biology that underlies that really criminal,

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: terrible behavior, should that be a cost for understanding it or not?

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: Okay, so in this case, he took the drugs, he killed the person you think he's pretty responsible.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: But how about somebody who kills somebody and it turns out in such a case a man with no

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: history, no history of criminal activity of any kind, shoved his wife out of a high window

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: to her death in the course of a standard argument.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: Then they discovered that he has a big tumor growing in the basal part of his frontal cortex.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: Now is the man responsible for what he did?

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: It's a really interesting question.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: And the more we understand about the neuroscience of our brains, what makes us do things which lets

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: us control impulses, what makes us murderous.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: There's a guy Kent Keel, University of Mexico, who's driving around in a big van with an MRI

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: scanner and he goes from penitentiary to penitentiary and he images the brains of serial murderers,

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: of psychopathic murderers.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: Okay?

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: And he's getting things that are pretty systematically different about their brains.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: And the question is, you know, what does that mean?

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: Does that mean those people were prone to do it for reasons of their early environment or

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: genes or some incident that happened?

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: If that's the case, do you forgive them differently?

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: Should we have a more different moral code or legal code as we discovered the brain basis

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: for some of these things?

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: Or is that irrelevant?

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: What counts as the action?

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: So this is kind of the thing you could think about, maybe discuss in your section some,

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: the more we understand about the human brain, the more we'll understand about behaviors

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: that we find terrible.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: And what does that mean about legal and moral decisions about how to punish those or think

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: about those?

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: So that's coming in your lifetime, because there's steady progress on this and how to think

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: about that is a really big question.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: So I'm going to talk with you today about research and psychology.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: And a funny thing about psychology, and I'll come back to this, is everybody is an amateur

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: psychologist.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: They have to be.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: If you deal with people, you're an amateur psychologist.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: Moreover, we live in a world with Oprah Winfrey and Dr. Phil, right?

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: So in case you're only an amateur one, you hear all kinds of stuff.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: Use paper, internet, magazines, everywhere you go, friends will tell you stuff about psychologists

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: to found this or psychologists to found that, right?

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: So you have your own intuitions to deal with people in everyday life.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: And here's a kind of a sentence from Time Magazine that drives psychologists crazy if you

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: take it seriously.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: So here's this literal sentence.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: Almost half of children of the divorces enter adulthood as worried, underachieving, self-deprecating,

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: sometimes angry young men and women.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: Okay.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: So that sounds kind of like a psychological study of some kind, kind of, right?

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: Somebody who wants to argue about the importance of divorce and breakdown of the family, this

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: is the kind of thing.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: So let's think about this for a moment.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: Almost half of children of divorce enter adulthood as worried.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: Are you guys worried about global warming?

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: A little.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: Are you worried about peace in the Middle East and how that influence is Middle East and

chunks.text: the US a little?

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: Are you worried about an economy that may not have jobs waiting for you?

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: A little, okay?

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: Who's not worried a little, a little bit of the time?

chunks.text: So almost, so it doesn't even tell you that half of children in the world, for example,

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: Because that's the only relevant question.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3

chunks.text: Underachieving?

chunks.text: Scientifically, okay?

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: I mean, most of you probably have had days where you've underachieved and days where you

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: have achieved pretty well.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: But how would you measure and prove that self-deprecating?

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: Well, sometimes things actually kind of nice of a person's modest, okay?

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: What's the difference between modest and self-deprecation?

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: And I like this one.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: Sometimes angry young men and women, right?

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: I mean, you know, I mean, it's, are you sometimes angry about something or another, okay?

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: And if you are, does that tell us that society is breaking down and your parents did something

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: bad that you're sometimes, I mean, who is a human being is not sometimes angry?

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: So here's a kind of psychology sounding sentence.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: On a serious topic, which is, you know, what is divorce, consequences of divorces and families?

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: You know, I should be thinking about things that encourage marriages to stay stable and

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: things like that.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: But there's really no information at all, right?

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: So from a research or science perspective, so I'm going to talk about a little bit, just

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: reminding you what science is.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: We're at a university, obviously, that thinks about science and does science all the time.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: That reminds you of what an experiment is, a very brief history of psychology experimentation.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: And then walk you through a few experiments that I find kind of interesting and then some

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: topics related to psychology.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: So let me start with this.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: How do you know what is true?

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: How do you know what is true that you take to be true?

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: What is the answer?

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: Everyone's looking at it.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: How do I know?

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: Please tell me.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: When people try to analyze this and you can think about it for your own self, one of them

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: is authority.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: Parents, teachers, textbooks, scientists, professors, and courses.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: We tell you this is the truth, right?

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: And if you remember the truth correctly, you get an A.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: So you know, authority's a huge source.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: I mean, it would be costly to go around and kind of have your own idea about everything.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: 12 inches in a foot.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: I'm not taking that piece of information without schedule, right?

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: I mean, you'd have to, you couldn't lead your life without kind of believing a lot of

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: stuff.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: But you know, you don't want to believe everything, right?

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: You just because you hear it repetition or tenacity.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: You hear something often enough and people tend to believe stuff.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: Operator, what's reasonable?

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: You think things through just by thought.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: What's possible, rational and so on.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: And you know, then maybe a fourth brand of knowledge to something is scientific analysis,

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: where we have hypotheses and we test them in some version of experiments or at least correlations

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: among data.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: Now there's another view, which is kind of that whatever we think makes something science

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: is what the scientific community decides as a community is true, right?

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: Roughly speaking, that is the mainstay of faculty at places like MIT and Stanford and Harvard

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: and so on and around the world at universities tell you this is how science works.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: You go, okay, that's how science works.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: And I remember some years ago, there was a lot of excitement about a satirical article

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: written by a scientist where he said, oh, this is all just societal authority.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: Scientists tell you what's true just because they believe in it just like, you know, sports

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: writers says the Celtics are the best.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: You don't really think that the sports writer knows scientifically the Celtics are the best,

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: right?

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: Okay.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: So, you know, so, and he wrote a paper saying that gravity is simply a consensus among scientists

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: and a particular view they're foisting on society, okay?

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: But none of us would want to really go up there and jump out the window going, well, gravity

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: is, you know, one person's opinion and here's my opinion, right?

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: Okay.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: So, but it's kind of interesting because, you know, for those of you who go to medical school,

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: for example, you learn lots of procedures and so on, the medical committee believes this

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: true, but there's not much scientific evidence behind it, but they have to use it every day.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: So you know, you're used to hearing about the scientific method.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: It applies to psychology in many ways, just like it applies to chemistry, physics or biology.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: We have to test things that are falsifiable.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: We have to deal in probabilities of outcomes in terms of statistics in some sense.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: The kinds of data we can have are descriptive or correlative or experimental.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: I'll talk about those in a moment.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: We are always trying to disprove the null hypothesis statistically as you know, which we can't

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: prove that something is true, but we can prove that it's not true.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: And then finally, like all fields of science, the most exciting, today's most exciting breakthroughs

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: are tomorrow's things that have to be apologized for and rediscovered, right?

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: Okay.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: So, making something breaking science is not that it's known with certainty.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: What's known with certainty is what's in your textbook, kind of.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: What makes something breaking science is, it moves the field forward.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: So what's the difference between the cutting edge and the bleeding edge is a very close call.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: So here's a cartoon that says, he's constantly proving his experiments wrong, okay?

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: And the guy's kind of glum looking over, he's proving the null hypothesis to be correct.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: Now, that's sort of funny for scientists.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: But it's sort of funny in the sense that, no, we don't really go around going.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: I hope, you know, with all my heart for this research project, I've worked on for the last five years,

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: that it will prove the null hypothesis.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: There's no difference between things I'm studying.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: In fact, scientists like all humans have agendas and hopes, like everybody else.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: So they observe hypothesized experiment, assist data, and it doesn't always go perfectly, right?

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: And the last thing I want to say is, I'm going to emphasize today a scientific approach to the human mind

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: and behavior, but of course, there's many other paths of knowledge for important things in our life,

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: religion, philosophy, arts, and so on.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: There's many things that science cannot address, and I don't want to pretend that it does.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: You know, that's not a problem too much when you think about chemistry.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: It's kind of an interesting problem when you think about psychology,

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: because when we think about happiness or values or things like that,

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: those topics cut across psychology and things like religion, philosophy, and topics like that.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: So what makes something an experiment is you have to have sort of two things conceptually, especially,

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: the dependent variable, what you measure, the outcome, and the independent variable, what you vary.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: Once you have those things in an experiment, you're kind of an experiment in a broad sense.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: So in psychology, it took a long time.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: It wasn't until the 1800s that people had kind of an approach to doing experiments in psychology.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: And figuring out, for example, how to use the simple measurement of time

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: to measure how long a mental operation lasts in your mind.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: So that's pretty cool.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: If you were to come to a fifth grader and say, how would you measure not a brick or a stone,

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: but how long a thought takes?

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: You'd say, well, that'd be pretty hard, but it turns out it's not as hard in a simple way.

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: So Wilhelm Wont, who big figure in psychology, first textbook in psychology,

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: first laboratory in psychology, did the following test of mental chronometry,

chunks.title: Lecture7.mp3
chunks.text: simply measuring how long it takes you to do things?
